hook: You already know how to describe a function like $f(x) = x^2$, which pairs each input with exactly one output. But what if you want to describe a more flexible pairing — like 'student $a$ is enrolled in course $b$', where one student can be in many courses and one course can have many students? A function can't capture that. How do we formally describe any kind of pairing or comparison between two sets?
concept: A relation is a way of describing which pairs of elements from two sets are 'connected' in some meaningful way — enrolled in, less than, divides, and so on. Formally, a binary relation $R$ from set $A$ to set $B$ is any subset of the Cartesian product $A \times B$; we write $a \, R \, b$ (or $(a, b) \in R$) to mean the pair is included. When $A = B$, we call $R$ a relation on $A$.
anatomy: Every binary relation $R$ from $A$ to $B$ has three distinguishable parts: the domain set $A$ (the left-hand elements that can appear as first coordinates), the codomain set $B$ (the right-hand elements that can appear as second coordinates), and the relation set $R \subseteq A \times B$ itself — the explicit collection of ordered pairs $(a, b)$ that satisfy the relationship. The ordered pair $(a, b)$ has a first component $a \in A$ and a second component $b \in B$, and the order matters: $(a, b) \in R$ does not imply $(b, a) \in R$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by clearly naming our two sets. $A$ will supply the first components of every ordered pair, and $B$ will supply the second components, so the full universe of possible pairs is $A \times B$, which has $3 \times 3 = 9$ elements.

We form the Cartesian product by pairing every element of $A$ with every element of $B$. This is the pool from which any relation $R$ from $A$ to $B$ must draw its pairs — $R$ can be any subset of these nine pairs, including the empty set or the full product itself.

worked_example.expression_latex: R = \{(a, b) \in A \times B \mid a \text{ divides } b\}
worked_example.description_latex: We now define a specific relation: $a \, R \, b$ whenever $a$ divides $b$ evenly. We check each pair — for instance, $1$ divides $2$, $4$, and $6$; $2$ divides $2$, $4$, and $6$; but $3$ divides only $6$ among the elements of $B$.

After testing all nine candidates we collect the ones that pass the divisibility test. Notice that $(3, 2)$ and $(3, 4)$ are excluded because $3 \nmid 2$ and $3 \nmid 4$, illustrating that $R$ is a proper subset of $A \times B$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Directed-graph (digraph) representation: draw nodes } 1,2,3 \text{ and } 2,4,6; \text{ draw an arrow from } a \text{ to } b \text{ for each } (a,b)\in R.
worked_example.description_latex: One powerful way to visualise a relation is as a directed graph: each element of $A$ and $B$ becomes a node, and each ordered pair in $R$ becomes a directed edge from the first component to the second. This makes it easy to see at a glance which elements are related and in which direction.

partial_example.description_latex: We are given these two sets and asked to find the Cartesian product $A \times B$ and then list the relation $R = \{(x, y) \in A \times B \mid y = 1\}$. Start by writing out every possible ordered pair in $A \times B$.
Here is the full Cartesian product — six pairs in total. Now you need to decide which of these six pairs satisfy the condition $y = 1$, and list only those pairs as the relation $R$.
Apply the rule $y = 1$: go through each pair in $A \times B$ and keep only those whose second component equals $1$. Write your answer as a set of ordered pairs.

The three pairs with second component $1$ form the relation. Notice that $R$ contains exactly one pair for each element of $A$, which means this particular relation is also a function — a good connection to what you already know.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \{0, 1, 2\}$. List all elements of $A \times A$.
Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and define the relation $R$ on $A$ by $R = \{(a,b) \mid a < b\}$. List all pairs in $R$.
Let $A = \{2, 3, 6\}$ and $B = \{1, 2, 3, 6\}$. Define $R = \{(a,b) \in A \times B \mid a \mid b\}$. List all pairs in $R$ and represent $R$ as a matrix (rows indexed by $A$, columns by $B$).
Let $A = \{1,2,3\}$ and $B = \{1,2,3\}$. How many distinct relations are there from $A$ to $B$? Recall that $|A \times B| = 9$.

A class has students $S = \{\text{Alice, Bob, Carol}\}$ and courses $C = \{\text{Math, CS, English}\}$. Alice takes Math and CS; Bob takes CS; Carol takes Math, CS, and English. Write the enrollment relation $R \subseteq S \times C$ as a set of ordered pairs, and state $|R|$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: R = \{(\text{Alice, Math}),(\text{Alice, CS}),(\text{Bob, CS}),(\text{Carol, Math}),(\text{Carol, CS}),(\text{Carol, English})\}; \quad |R|=6

common_mistakes: Students write $(b, a)$ instead of $(a, b)$ when describing a relation from $A$ to $B$, forgetting that ordered pairs are directional — $(a, b) \in A \times B$ means $a$ comes from $A$ and $b$ comes from $B$, so always write the element from the domain set first.

Students confuse a relation with a function, assuming every element of $A$ must appear exactly once as a first component in $R$; in reality, an element of $A$ can appear in many pairs, in one pair, or in no pairs at all — $R$ is simply any subset of $A \times B$.

When representing a relation as a Boolean matrix, students sometimes place a $1$ at position $(i,j)$ to mean the $j$-th row element is related to the $i$-th column element, reversing rows and columns; always index rows by the domain set and columns by the codomain set, so entry $(i,j) = 1$ means $a_i \, R \, b_j$.
untested_variants: Composition of two relations $R \subseteq A \times B$ and $S \subseteq B \times C$ to form $S \circ R \subseteq A \times C$.
Determining whether a relation on a single set is reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric, or transitive.
Finding the inverse relation $R^{-1} = \{(b,a) \mid (a,b) \in R\}$ and describing its properties.
Representing a relation on a single set as a directed graph (digraph) and reading properties directly from the digraph structure.
